2604.25716.title: Cross-Lingual Jailbreak Detection via Semantic Codebooks
2604.25716.paper_summary: Safety mechanisms for large language models (LLMs) remain predominantly English-centric, creating systematic vulnerabilities in multilingual deployment. Prior work shows that translating malicious prompts into other languages can substantially increase jailbreak success rates, exposing a structural cross-lingual security gap. We investigate whether such attacks can be mitigated through language-agnostic semantic similarity without retraining or language-specific adaptation. Our approach compares multilingual query embeddings against a fixed English codebook of jailbreak prompts, operating as a training-free external guardrail for black-box LLMs. We conduct a systematic evaluation across four languages, two translation pipelines, four safety benchmarks, three embedding models, and three target LLMs (Qwen, Llama, GPT-3.5). Our results reveal two distinct regimes of cross-lingual transfer. On curated benchmarks containing canonical jailbreak templates, semantic similarity generalizes reliably across languages, achieving near-perfect separability (AUC up to 0.99) and substantial reductions in absolute attack success rates under strict low-false-positive constraints. However, under distribution shift - on behaviorally diverse and heterogeneous unsafe benchmarks - separability degrades markedly (AUC $\approx$ 0.60-0.70), and recall in the security-critical low-FPR regime drops across all embedding models.
2604.25716.paper_summary_zh: 大型語言模型（LLMs）的安全機制仍然主要以英語為中心，這在多語言部署中創造了系統性的脆弱性。先前的研究顯示，將惡意提示翻譯成其他語言可以顯著提高越獄成功率，暴露出結構性的跨語言安全漏洞。我們調查了是否可以通過語言無關的語義相似性來減輕這類攻擊，而無需重新訓練或語言特定的調整。我們的方法將多語言查詢嵌入與固定的英語越獄提示代碼本進行比較，作為黑箱LLMs的無需訓練的外部護欄。我們在四種語言、兩個翻譯管道、四個安全基準、三個嵌入模型和三個目標LLMs（Qwen、Llama、GPT-3.5）之間進行了系統評估。我們的結果揭示了兩種不同的跨語言轉移模式。在包含典範越獄模板的精選基準上，語義相似性在不同語言之間可靠地概括，實現了接近完美的可分性（AUC高達0.99），並在嚴格的低假陽性約束下顯著降低了絕對攻擊成功率。然而，在分佈轉移下——在行為多樣且異質的安全基準上——可分性顯著下降（AUC約為0.60-0.70），並且在安全關鍵的低假陽性率範疇內，所有嵌入模型的召回率均有所下降。
2604.25716.author: Shirin Alanova et.al.
2604.25716.authors: Shirin Alanova, Bogdan Minko, Sabrina Sadiekh, Evgeniy Kokuykin
